mission.title: Install and Configure Istio on Kubernetes
mission.description: Istio is an open source service mesh that provides a uniform way to connect, secure, control, and observe services across microservices. Installing Istio allows you to manage traffic flow, enforce policies, and aggregate telemetry data seamlessly in your Kubernetes environment.

steps.title: Add the Istio Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Istio Helm repository to your local Helm client to access the Istio charts.
```bash
helm repo add istio https://istio-release.storage.googleapis.com/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Istio base components
steps.description:
Install the base components of Istio using Helm. This is a prerequisite for installing the Istio control plane.
```bash
helm install istio-base istio/base -n istio-system --create-namespace --version 1.29.0
```

steps.title: Install Istiod control plane
steps.description:
Install the Istiod control plane, which manages the service mesh configuration and traffic management.
```bash
helm install istiod istio/istiod -n istio-system --version 1.29.0
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Istio pods are running in the istio-system namespace to confirm a successful installation.
```bash
kubectl get pods -n istio-system
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits for Istio components
steps.description:
Set resource limits for the Istio components to ensure they run efficiently within your cluster. Modify the values.yaml file or use the --set flag during installation.
```bash
helm upgrade istiod istio/istiod -n istio-system --set resources.limits.cpu=500m --set resources.limits.memory=512Mi --version 1.29.0
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Istio will show the Istio pods running in the istio-system namespace. You can also verify that the resource limits have been applied by checking the pod specifications.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add istio https://istio-release.storage.googleapis.com/charts
helm repo update
helm upgrade istiod istio/istiod -n istio-system --set resources.limits.cpu=500m --set resources.limits.memory=512Mi --version 1.29.0

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release for Istiod
uninstall.description: Uninstall the Istiod control plane from the istio-system namespace using Helm.

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description: Remove the Istio Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and any persistent resources associated with the installation.

uninstall.title: Remove the istio-system namespace and verify
uninstall.description: Delete the istio-system namespace and verify that all resources have been removed.

upgrade.title: Backup current state of Istio
upgrade.description: Before upgrading, back up the current configuration and custom resources of Istio.

upgrade.title: Update the Helm repository and run the upgrade command
upgrade.description: Update the Istio Helm repository and upgrade the Istiod release to the latest version.

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description: Check that the Istio pods are running the new version in the istio-system namespace to confirm a successful upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description: This issue occurs when a pod fails to start repeatedly. Check the pod logs for errors using the command: `kubectl logs <pod-name> -n istio-system`.

troubleshooting.title: Istio components not starting
troubleshooting.description: If Istio components are not starting, check for resource constraints or misconfigurations. Use `kubectl describe pod <pod-name> -n istio-system` to diagnose the issue.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description: If services are not reachable, ensure that the Istio ingress gateway is configured correctly. Verify with `kubectl get svc -n istio-system`.

troubleshooting.title: Configuration changes not taking effect
troubleshooting.description: If configuration changes are not being applied, check if the Istio configuration resources are correctly defined and applied. Use `kubectl get virtualservices -n istio-system` to review the current configuration.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher, along with Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
